name: instruction-padding-protection
description: Detects and blocks instruction padding attacks where malicious actors add excessive trailing bytes to bypass security checks. Uses statistical anomaly detection to minimize false positives while catching padding-based exploits.

rules.name: Critical: Excessive Instruction Padding
rules.message: Transaction blocked: Instruction contains excessive padding ({{padding:max_padding_bytes}} bytes). This pattern is commonly used in padding attacks to bypass security checks.

rules.name: High: Repeated Padding Pattern Detected
rules.message: Transaction blocked: Instruction contains repeated padding pattern (e.g., 0x00 or 0xFF bytes). This suggests a malicious attempt to hide or obfuscate instruction data.

rules.name: High: Suspicious Padding Ratio
rules.message: Transaction blocked: Instruction padding ratio ({{padding:max_padding_ratio}}x) exceeds safe threshold. The instruction data is significantly larger than expected for its type.

rules.name: Medium: Multiple Instructions with Suspicious Padding
rules.message: Warning: Transaction contains {{padding:suspicious_instruction_count}} instructions with suspicious padding. Review the transaction details carefully before proceeding.

rules.name: Low: Single Instruction with Moderate Padding
rules.message: Advisory: Instruction contains moderate padding ({{padding:max_padding_bytes}} bytes). This may be legitimate (e.g., Token-2022 extensions) but warrants review.

notes: This ruleset protects against instruction padding attacks while minimizing false positives.
Token-2022 extensions are allowed up to 512 bytes of additional data.
Anchor instructions are allowed up to 512 bytes total size.
Repeated byte patterns (0x00, 0xFF) are flagged as highly suspicious.
Padding ratio checks catch attacks that add proportionally excessive data.
false_positive_mitigation: Token-2022 extensions (TransferFee, Metadata, etc.) are explicitly allowed
Reasonable buffers are provided for legitimate variability
Statistical thresholds are based on real-world transaction patterns
Only extreme anomalies trigger blocks; moderate cases trigger warnings
